title: Functional Programming
summary: Write cleaner code using lambda expressions and the Stream API

sections.title: Lambdas as Anonymous Coworkers
sections.content: Imagine you have a temp worker who shows up, does exactly one task you specify, and leaves without you ever knowing their name. That's what a lambda expression is: a nameless function you can pass around like a value. Before lambdas, Java required you to create an entire class just to pass one piece of behavior. Now you can write (x, y) -> x + y and Java understands it as 'take x and y and return their sum'. Lambdas work hand-in-hand with functional interfaces — interfaces with exactly one abstract method, like Runnable, Comparator, and Consumer.

sections.title: Using Lambdas with the Stream API
sections.content: This code filters a list of products to find inexpensive ones using streams and lambdas

sections.title: Stream Pipeline: Map, Filter, Reduce
sections.content: The Stream API lets you process collections as a pipeline, like an assembly line in a factory. Filter removes items you don't want (like a quality inspector rejecting defective products). Map transforms each item (like a machine that paints each product red). Reduce combines all items into one result (like a scale that weighs all products together). The beauty is that these operations chain together: you start with a collection, filter it, map it, and collect the results — all in one readable expression. This replaces many verbose for-loops with concise, intent-revealing code.

sections.title: Modifying external variables inside lambdas
sections.content: Most common mistake: trying to modify a local variable from inside a lambda, like int count = 0; list.forEach(item -> count++);. Local variables used in lambdas must be effectively final (not reassigned). Fix: use an atomic counter or a stream reduction instead. Wrong: int sum = 0; numbers.forEach(n -> sum += n);. Correct: int sum = numbers.stream().reduce(0, (a, b) -> a + b);

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: Given a list of Employee objects with name (String) and salary (double) fields, use the Stream API to find the average salary of all employees earning more than $50,000. Hint: combine filter with mapToDouble and average.
hints: What stream method selects only employees above $50,000?
How do you convert a stream of employees to a stream of their salaries?
What terminal operation calculates the average of a numeric stream?
